params.description: Equal groups appear. 3 bags, each containing 4 apples. Bags clearly separated.
Equation Builder appears. Template shows three blanks with multiplication and equals signs. Tile palette shows numbers 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12, 14.

beats.text: You know how to do this. Build the equation shown by the picture.
Build the equation.

validator.description: Student placed 3 × 4 = 12, correct

params.description: Equation tiles lock into place confirming 3 × 4 = 12.

beats.text: Right. 3 groups of 4. 3 times 4 equals 12. You've got equation building down.

params.description: The equals sign subtly highlights.

beats.text: But now, let's look more closely at that equals sign.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

beats.text: Check the picture. Count the bags, then count what's in each bag.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

params.description: The 3 bags highlight.

beats.text: I see 3 bags. That's the first number. Now count how many apples are in one bag. That's the second number. Then figure out the total.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Bags highlight sequentially: 1, 2, 3.

beats.text: Let me show you. I count the bags: 1, 2, 3. That's 3 groups. Now I count one bag: 1, 2, 3, 4 apples. So 3 times 4. And the product? 3 fours: 4, 8, 12. The equation is 3 times 4 equals 12.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 3 bags, each containing 4 apples. Bags clearly separated.
Equation Builder showing completed equation 3 × 4 = 12. Equals sign highlighted.

params.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 3 containers with 4 apples each.
Equation Builder displaying 3 × 4 = 12, equation complete from prior section.
Equals sign highlights in the equation 3 × 4 = 12.

beats.text: See this sign right here? The equals sign. Here's something important about it.

elements.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 3 containers with 4 apples each.
Equation Builder showing 3 × 4 = 12, equals sign highlighted.

params.description: Equals sign glows and pulses in the equation.

beats.text: It means SAME VALUE AS. 3 times 4 has the same value as 12. Not the answer is. Equals means same value.

elements.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 3 containers with 4 apples each.
Equation Builder showing 3 × 4 = 12, equals sign pulsing.

beats.text: And since both sides have the same value, we can flip them around.

params.description: Equation rearranges from 3 × 4 = 12 to 12 = 3 × 4. Original fades, reversed orientation stays.

beats.text: 12 equals 3 times 4. Same equation. Same value on both sides. Just written differently.

elements.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 3 containers with 4 apples each.
Equation Builder showing 12 = 3 × 4, reversed orientation.

params.description: Static equation 3 × 4 = 12 appears above the reversed form.
Equation Builder shows 12 = 3 × 4, reversed orientation, below the original.

beats.text: Both of these are correct. The equals sign works both ways because it means same value as.

elements.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 3 containers with 4 apples each.
Static equation 3 × 4 = 12 displayed above.
Equation Builder showing 12 = 3 × 4, reversed orientation, below the original.

params.description: Equal Groups apples visualization clears.
Static equation 3 × 4 = 12 clears.
Equal Groups visualization appears showing 6 boxes with 7 crayons in each.
Equation Builder resets with empty template, tile palette available.

beats.text: Your turn. Build the equation for these boxes.
Build the equation for these boxes.

validator.description: Student placed 6 × 7 = 42, correct

beats.text: You got it. 6 times 7 equals 42. Six boxes, 7 in each, same value as 42.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

beats.text: Count the boxes. Then count what's in each box.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

params.description: Groups and items slots highlight in the equation template.

beats.text: Look at the boxes. How many boxes? That's the first number. Count the crayons in one box. That's the second number. Then skip count by 7s: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42. That's the product.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially as guide counts: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.

beats.text: Let me show you. I count the boxes: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Six boxes. That's my first number. Now I count one box: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. Seven crayons. That's my second number. 6 times 7. And the product? Skip count by 7s six times: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42. The equation is 6 times 7 equals 42. That's how equal groups connect to multiplication.

elements.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 6 boxes with 7 crayons in each.
Equation Builder with template, place_tile tool active, tile palette visible.

params.description: Reversed equation 42 = 6 × 7 appears below the student's built equation.

beats.text: And we can also write it this way. Same value, both sides.

elements.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 6 boxes with 7 crayons in each.
Equation Builder showing student's completed equation 6 × 7 = 42.
Reversed equation 42 = 6 × 7 displayed below.

params.description: Equal Groups crayons visualization clears.
Reversed equation 42 = 6 × 7 clears.
Equal Groups visualization appears showing 5 bags with 9 stars in each.
Equation Builder resets with empty template, new tile palette available.

beats.text: Same idea. Count the bags, count what's inside, figure out the product. Build the equation.
Build the equation.

validator.description: Student placed 5 × 9 = 45, correct

beats.text: Right. 5 times 9 equals 45. Five bags, 9 in each, same value as 45.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

beats.text: Look closely. How many bags? How many stars in each?

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

params.description: All 5 bags highlight.

beats.text: Count the bags: 5. Count the stars in one bag: 9. So 5 times 9. Skip count by 9s: 9, 18, 27, 36, 45. Place those three numbers.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: Bags highlight one at a time as guide counts them.

beats.text: Here's how it works. I count the bags: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Five bags total. Then I pick one bag and count the stars inside: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. Nine stars. So I have 5 groups of 9. 5 times 9. To find the product, I skip count by 9s: 9, 18, 27, 36, 45. The answer is 5 times 9 equals 45. Five bags, 9 in each, same value as 45.

elements.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 5 bags with 9 stars in each.
Equation Builder with template, place_tile tool active, tile palette visible.

params.description: Reversed equation 45 = 5 × 9 appears below the student's built equation.

beats.text: Both ways work.

elements.description: Equal Groups visualization showing 5 bags with 9 stars in each.
Equation Builder showing student's completed equation 5 × 9 = 45.
Reversed equation 45 = 5 × 9 displayed below.

params.description: Equal groups visualization appears. 3 boxes, 8 cookies in each box.
Equation Builder appears with REVERSED template: product slot = groups slot × items slot. Tile palette: 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 11, 16, 20, 21, 24.

beats.text: Here's something different. The template is flipped. Product comes FIRST this time. Remember, equals means same value as, so the product can go on either side. Build this equation with the product first.
Build the equation: __ = __ × __

validator.description: Student placed 24 = 3 × 8, correct reversed form

beats.text: Right. 24 equals 3 times 8. Product on the left, multiplication expression on the right. Same value on both sides.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Count the boxes, count what's inside each, then look at the total. Product goes first in this template.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

params.description: Boxes highlight sequentially: 1, 2, 3 boxes total.

beats.text: Let's count together. 3 boxes. Each box has 8 cookies. 3 times 8 is 24. The reversed template puts 24 first, then the equal sign, then 3 times 8.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates placing tiles: 24 in product slot, 3 in groups slot, 8 in items slot.

beats.text: Let me show you. I count 3 boxes. Each box has 8 cookies. 3 times 8 equals 24. In the reversed template, the product 24 goes first. Then equals. Then 3 times 8. Same value on both sides.

elements.description: Equal groups visualization. 3 boxes, 8 cookies in each.
Equation Builder with reversed template showing completed equation 24 = 3 × 8.

params.description: Equal groups visualization appears. 4 bags, 6 marbles in each bag. Containers visible and clearly separated.
Equation Builder appears with standard template. Three blanks: groups, items per group, total. Tile palette shows 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 10, 16, 18, 24, 28.

beats.text: One more with bags. Build the equation.
Build the equation that matches the picture.

validator.description: Student placed 4, 6, and 24 to build 4 × 6 = 24, correct

beats.text: Right. 4 times 6 equals 24. Four bags, 6 in each, same value as 24.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on first attempt

beats.text: Check the picture. Count the bags. Count what's in each bag.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles on second attempt

params.description: All 4 bags highlight to show the group count.

beats.text: Here's a hint: I see 4 bags. That's 4 groups. Count the marbles in one bag. That number goes in the middle. Then skip-count by that number 4 times to find the product.

validator.description: Student placed incorrect tiles three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates placing tiles: 4 in groups slot, 6 in items slot, 24 in product slot.

beats.text: Let me show you how this works. First, I count the bags. That's the number of groups: 4. Then I count the marbles in one bag: 6. So I've got 4 groups of 6. Now I skip-count by 6 four times: 6, 12, 18, 24. The product is 24. The equation is 4 times 6 equals 24. Four bags of 6 marbles means the same value as 24 total marbles.

elements.description: Equal groups visualization. 4 bags, 6 marbles in each.
Equation Builder showing completed equation 4 × 6 = 24.

params.description: Static equation appears below the builder displaying reversed form: 24 = 4 × 6.

beats.text: And we can write it this way too. Same value.

elements.description: Equal groups visualization. 4 bags, 6 marbles in each.
Equation Builder showing completed equation 4 × 6 = 24.
Static equation displaying reversed form 24 = 4 × 6.

params.description: Equal groups visualization appears showing 3 rows of books on shelves. Each row contains 9 books arranged horizontally. Books clearly separated into 3 distinct rows.
Equation Builder appears with three blank slots and tile palette showing numbers 3, 4, 8, 9, 10, 12, 24, 27, 30, 36.

beats.text: Equal groups don't just come in bags and boxes. They're everywhere. Rows of books. Stacks of plates. Groups of animals. The math works the same way.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 3 rows of 9 books on shelves, horizontal arrangement, containers visible.
Equation Builder with empty template and tile palette available.

beats.text: Look at these shelves. I see rows of books. Each row is a group, just like each bag was a group.

params.description: Rows highlight sequentially: first row, then second row, then third row.

beats.text: 1, 2, 3. Three rows. That's 3 groups. And in each row?

params.description: Items in first row highlight sequentially while counting.

beats.text: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. Nine books. So I've got 3 rows of 9. Same pattern: groups of items.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 3 rows of 9 books, first row items highlighted from counting demonstration.
Equation Builder with empty template.

beats.text: 3 times 9. And the product? Skip-count by 9s: 9, 18, 27.

params.description: Guide demonstration: tiles 3, 9, and 27 place into equation builder slots to show 3 × 9 = 27.

beats.text: 3 times 9 equals 27. Same equation building, different picture.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 3 rows of 9 books on shelves.
Equation Builder displaying completed equation 3 × 9 = 27.

params.description: Reversed form equation appears below builder: 27 = 3 × 9.

beats.text: Both ways, same value.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 3 rows of 9 books on shelves.
Equation Builder displaying completed equation 3 × 9 = 27.
Static equation showing reversed form 27 = 3 × 9.

params.description: Image A: 3 shelves with 6 jars on each shelf. Equal groups — 3 containers, 6 items per container.
params.label: A

params.description: Image B: 4 plates with different numbers of cookies — 5, 6, 5, 2. Unequal groups — same containers, different amounts.
params.label: B

params.description: Image C: 2 equal stacks of 9 books. Equal groups — 2 containers, 9 items per container.
params.label: C

params.description: Image D: 3 groups of animals — 7 ducks, 4 ducks, 7 ducks. Unequal groups — middle group differs.
params.label: D

beats.text: Equal groups are everywhere, but not every group of things is an equal group. Look at these four pictures. Each picture has 18 items in total. Which ones show equal groups, the same number in every group?
Select ALL the pictures that show equal groups.

validator.description: Student selected A and C, both correct — A shows 3 shelves with 6 jars each, C shows 2 stacks of 9 books

beats.text: Right. The first picture has 3 shelves with 6 on each, equal groups. The third picture has 2 stacks of 9, equal groups. We can write multiplication equations for these. The other two? The groups are not equal, different amounts in different groups. We cannot write a multiplication equation for those.

validator.description: Student did not select both A and C on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Equal groups means the same number in every group. Look at each picture again.

validator.description: Student did not select both A and C on second attempt

params.description: Image A highlights — 3 shelves, 6 jars on each shelf.
Image C highlights — 2 stacks, 9 books in each stack.

beats.text: Count carefully. The first picture has 6 jars on EACH shelf. The third picture has 9 books in EACH stack. Same count in every group — that's equal groups. Are there any others?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System demonstrates counting Image A: shelf 1 shows 6 jars, shelf 2 shows 6 jars, shelf 3 shows 6 jars.

beats.text: Image A: I count the first shelf — 6 jars. Second shelf — 6 jars. Third shelf — 6 jars. Every shelf has the same number. That's equal groups.

params.description: System demonstrates counting Image C: stack 1 shows 9 books, stack 2 shows 9 books.

beats.text: Image C: First stack — 9 books. Second stack — 9 books. Same number in both. Equal groups. A and C show equal groups. We can write multiplication equations for pictures with equal groups.

elements.description: Image A: 3 shelves with 6 jars each. Selected.
elements.label: A

elements.description: Image B: 4 plates with unequal cookie counts.
elements.label: B

elements.description: Image C: 2 stacks of 9 books. Selected.
elements.label: C

elements.description: Image D: 3 groups of ducks with unequal counts.
elements.label: D

params.description: Equal groups visualization appears. 8 stacks of 4 plates. Plates are clearly stacked and separated.
Equation Builder appears with pre-filled equation: 8 × 4 = ☐. The ☐ is visible in the product slot. Palette shows answer tiles: 28, 30, 32, 36.

beats.text: 8 stacks of 4 plates. These are equal groups so we can write a multiplication equation to represent the picture. The expression part is done. 8 times 4. But see that box where the product goes? That's the unknown. The number we need to figure out. Find the total and place it.
8 × 4 = ☐. Find the product. Place the number.

validator.description: Student placed 32 in the product slot, correct

params.description: The 32 tile locks into the product slot, equation now complete: 8 × 4 = 32.

beats.text: Right. 8 times 4 equals 32. You found the unknown. 32 is the product. 8 stacks, 4 in each, same value as 32.

validator.description: Student placed 28. Specific error: chose first palette option without calculating

params.description: All 8 stacks highlight.

beats.text: Look at all 8 stacks. Count how many plates are in one stack. Then skip-count by that number 8 times to find the product.

validator.description: Student placed 36. Specific error: added instead of multiplying (8 + 4 + ... pattern or miscounting)

params.description: First stack highlights.

beats.text: Count one stack: 4 plates. Now skip-count by 4s for all 8 stacks: 4, 8, 12... Keep going until you've counted all 8 stacks.

validator.description: Student selected any other wrong answer on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Count the plates in one stack, then skip-count by that number 8 times.

validator.description: Student selected any other wrong answer on second attempt

params.description: First stack of 4 plates highlights.

beats.text: Here's what helps: one stack has 4 plates. So skip-count by 4s, eight times: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32. What number did you land on?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System counts through all 8 stacks sequentially: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32. Each stack highlights as it is counted.

beats.text: Let me show you. I count one stack: 4 plates. Then I skip-count by 4s for all 8 stacks. 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32. I counted 8 times and landed on 32. The product is 32 because 8 stacks of 4 equals 32 total.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 8 stacks of 4 plates, plates clearly stacked and separated.
Equation Builder displaying completed equation 8 × 4 = 32. Product slot filled with 32.

params.description: Equal groups visualization shows 5 clusters of 7 sheep in a field. Sheep grouped by natural spatial proximity with clear gaps between clusters. No circles or containers. Each cluster contains exactly 7 sheep.
Equation Builder appears with reversed template. Pre-filled with 5 × 7 on right side. Product slot (left side) empty, awaiting student input. Tile palette shows 12, 30, 35, 42.

beats.text: Look at this field. See the sheep? They're grouped together equally. 5 groups, 7 in each. The equation is flipped. Unknown first this time. But equals still means same value as. Figure out the product and place it.
☐ = 5 × 7. What is the product?

validator.description: Student placed 35 in the product slot, correct answer

beats.text: Right. 35 equals 5 times 7. The unknown was 35. Same value on both sides.

validator.description: Student placed a wrong tile on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Skip-count by 7s: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35. How many times did you count?

validator.description: Student placed a wrong tile on second attempt

params.description: Clusters highlight one at a time while skip-counting: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35.

beats.text: Watch the clusters highlight as we skip-count by 7. First cluster: 7. Second: 14. Third: 21. Fourth: 28. Fifth: 35. Five groups of 7 equals what total?

validator.description: Student placed a wrong tile on third attempt. System models the correct answer.

params.description: All 5 clusters highlight sequentially while counting: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35.
Tile 35 moves into the product slot. Equation completes: 35 = 5 × 7.

beats.text: Let me show you. I skip-count by 7 five times: 7, 14, 21, 28, 35. Five groups of 7 is 35 total. The unknown is 35. That is the product, and it is the same value as 5 times 7.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 5 clusters of 7 sheep grouped by spatial proximity in a field. No containers visible. 35 total sheep.
Equation Builder displaying completed reversed equation 35 = 5 × 7. All slots filled.

params.description: Equal groups visualization appears showing 4 faded clusters of 5 birds each. Total label displays '20 birds total.' Clusters are not yet revealed.
Equation Builder appears with template showing ☐ × 5 = 20. First factor slot is blank; second factor (5) and product (20) are pre-filled.

beats.text: Here's the new unknown. I know there are 5 birds in each group. And I know the total is 20 birds. But I don't know how many groups. That's the unknown.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 4 faded clusters of 5 birds. Total label: '20 birds total.'
Equation Builder showing template ☐ × 5 = 20. First factor unknown.

beats.text: Let me read the sentence: HOW MANY groups of 5 equals 20 total? I need to figure out how many groups of 5 make 20.

elements.description: Equal groups showing 4 faded clusters of 5 birds. Total label: '20 birds total.'
Equation Builder showing template ☐ × 5 = 20.

beats.text: I can skip-count by 5s. I know how to do that. Watch: 5...

params.description: First cluster of 5 birds reveals and highlights. Running count displays '5'.

params.description: Second cluster of 5 birds reveals and highlights. Running count updates to '10'.

params.description: Fourth cluster of 5 birds reveals and highlights. Running count updates to '20'.

params.description: Equation Builder appears with pre-filled template: ☐ × 10 = 40. First factor slot empty, second factor 10, product 40.

☐ × 10 = 40. Build groups of 10 until the total reaches 40.

Equal Groups in Construction Mode. 2 empty groups displayed, group count locked at 2. Running total: 0 / 10.

beats.text: Here's the equation. 2 groups of how many cats equals 10. We need to build the groups. Let's add equal items to the 2 groups until you reach 10. Add 5 to each group so the total reaches 10.
2 × ☐ = 10. Add 5 cats to each of the 2 groups to make 10 total.

Equation completes: 2 × 5 = 10.

beats.text: Right. 2 times 5 equals 10. You started with the equation and built the answer. Now we can skip count by 5s two times: 5, 10. That's the total of 10. Each side of the equation has the same value.

params.description: Equation Builder displays 5 × ☐ = 30. First factor 5 and product 30 filled; second factor slot empty.

beats.text: Here's the equation. 5 groups of something equals 30. Build the groups. Add items until you hit 30. Keep them equal!
5 × ☐ = 30. Build 5 equal groups that make 30 total.

beats.text: You hit 30! But check: are the groups equal? Multiplication needs equal groups. Adjust so each group has the same number.

validator.description: Student built equal groups but total is not 30

beats.text: Equal groups, great! But the total isn't 30 yet. Add more to each group (or remove some) and watch the total.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System demonstrates adding items to each group sequentially. Each group gets 6 items. Running total increments: 6, 12, 18, 24, 30. All groups highlight to show equality.

beats.text: Let me show you. 5 groups need to make 30 total. I add 6 to each group: 6, 12, 18, 24, 30. Each group has the same amount, 6, and the total is 30. 5 times 6 equals 30.

elements.description: Equation Builder displaying 5 × ☐ = 30 with second factor slot empty.
Equal Groups in building mode. 5 containers with 6 items each. Running total: 30. set_items_per_container tool active.

params.description: Second factor slot fills with 6. Equation Builder now shows complete equation: 5 × 6 = 30.
Static equation appears displaying reversed form: 30 = 5 × 6.

elements.description: Equation Builder displaying completed equation 5 × 6 = 30.
Equal Groups showing 5 containers with 6 items each. Total: 30.
Static equation showing reversed form: 30 = 5 × 6.

beats.text: You built equations with bags, rows, stacks, and groups. You found unknown numbers in different positions. Let's see what you know.
